original_abstract: This chapter examines the historical evolution of central-local government relations in Japan through the lenses of administration, finance, and politics. By employing a conceptual framework of "interfusion" and "separation," it clarifies the distinct characteristics defining the Japanese intergovernmental landscape. During its modernization phase, Japan looked toward European models and ultimately adopted an interfusion system, in which the central government delegates policy implementation to local authorities to ensure national standards. Throughout the 20th century, this relationship shifted through phases of wartime functional centralization, postwar decentralization under the Allied Occupation, and the more recent decentralization reforms initiated in the 1990s. Despite these shifts, the fundamental quality of interfusion has been preserved and even bolstered by large-scale municipal mergers and sophisticated fiscal adjustment mechanisms. Consequently, while Japan’s local governments account for a exceptionally high proportion of total public activity by international standards, this scale is fundamentally sustained by an integrated, interfusion-type relationship with the central state.
humanized_abstract: This chapter focuses on the development of the relationship between the central and local government in Japan, particularly in relation to the administration, finance and politics, and explains in what way their intergovernmental system is characterized by the interpenetration and differentiation of the central and local state bureaucracy. Japan adopted the European system of bureaucracy and therefore the interfusion model at the time of its modernization. This relationship has developed in various ways throughout the 20th century, such as wartime centralization, the post-1945 decentralization policy implemented by the Occupation forces, and the 1990s reform of decentralization. So, in spite of all these changes, the interfusion between the central and local state bureaucracy has not only been maintained but also reinforced. This is due to a number of structural factors, including the nationwide amalgamation of municipalities, and the sophisticated methods of fiscal harmonization. This means that local government is carrying out a very high proportion of public activities, a figure which is even higher than that of many other OECD countries, and which is therefore fully based on the interpenetration of the local state bureaucracy and the central state in an interfusion type of relationship.
